text: The surgeon gains access to the retroactive space on the patient's right side using a blunt trocar under direct visualization. The space is created effectively, followed by the placement of ports and docking of the robotic system to initiate the crossover procedure.

The surgeon evaluates the crossover area, identifying the rectus muscle by its color change. The striations of the linea alba are visible, abruptly ending at the location being shown, suggesting a potential area for crossover.

The surgeon observes the absence of the rectus muscle in the area, revealing only subcutaneous fat without an anterior sheath during the crossover procedure. This visual absence can be confusing when distinguishing between the linea alba and the subcutaneous space.

The surgeon observes the right side of the medial aspect of the linea alba, noting well-defined striations. Muscle fibers are visible, likely remnants from a previous surgery, as the dissection continues in this area.

The surgeon identifies the mesh located medially and decides to approach dissection above it to avoid the peritoneum. A vessel is identified and preserved, serving as a reference point during the surgery.
The surgeon identifies a suitable dissection plane along the linea alba, maintaining alignment to ensure precision and avoid deviation from the intended surgical path.
The surgeon assesses the lateral extent of the dissection, avoiding neurovascular bundles that may have been compromised, using the previously identified vessel as a reference point.

The surgeon uses kidney dissectors to elevate the mesh with the left hand, applying tension to reveal the tar space. The dissectors absorb blood, enhancing visibility of the plane. The surgeon exchanges soaked dissectors, which resemble caterpillars, to maintain effective tension and visibility.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, navigating past troublesome areas. The mesh is visible, with disrupted anterior tissue planes, requiring careful intra-abdominal evaluation through the rent to maintain spatial orientation.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon encounters difficulty locating the epigastric vessels, which are typically used to guide the trajectory towards the inguinal region. Several surgical clips are visible at the base, near the round ligament, indicating previous vessel sacrifice.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for hematomas, identifying a visible puddle of fluid. The assistant PA is seen preparing the mesh while the surgeon uses suction to manage the fluid accumulation.

The surgeon places a tailored mesh into the surgical site, deploying VistaSeal for hemostasis and space inclusion. The abdomen is insufflated, and a laparoscopic view confirms no breaks in the peritoneum or posterior sheath. A laparoscopic tap block is performed circumferentially on both sides to secure the mesh and apply suction.
